bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, 'Original Documents, Illustrating the Arts, &c. of the Middle Ages: Early English Artistical Receipts,' The Archaeological Journal 1 (1844): 152-155.
bibliographic.title: Original Documents, Illustrating the Arts, &c. of the Middle Ages: Early English Artistical Receipts

dateStatements.label: Article and journal-volume year
dateStatements.notes: The separately modeled completed bound carrier has an 1845 title page.

entities.description: The immediate source edition for CME00136: a four-page article under the recurring 'Original Documents' heading, with a modern introduction, folio labels, twelve selected Middle English recipe/advice units from Sloane MS 73, twelve explanatory footnotes, one reproduction of a marginal vessel drawing, and the signature T. WRIGHT.
entities.label: Thomas Wright, Early English Artistical Receipts (1844)
notes: The printed order is f. 173v, f. 138v, f. 138r, f. 213r, f. 213v, f. 215v and therefore is neither continuous nor ascending manuscript folio order.
The article begins at the top of printed p. 152 and ends with T. WRIGHT. and footnotes at the foot of p. 155; p. 156 begins a separate proceedings section.

rights.basis: The article was published in London in 1844 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.
rights.component: Wright's 1844 editorial selection, introduction, notes, figure reproduction, and typography

access.accessMethod: Exact catalog record, exact-shelfmark catalog search, current digitised-manuscripts guidance, and public web/repository searches checked for a complete authorized object
access.format: No complete public target-manuscript image resource established
notes: The exact record's Digitised Content field is empty and exposes no thumbnail, viewer, or IIIF link. Exact-shelfmark searches found no authorized complete public Sloane MS 73 facsimile.
This is a dated negative access finding, not proof that unpublished, internal, selected, legacy, or privately held photography does not exist. No restricted or selected manuscript images were cached.

access.accessMethod: Request the exact shelfmark with a valid Reader Pass; consult manuscripts and archives in London
notes: Readers who registered or renewed after 21 March 2024 may request in advance; older passes require renewal. Availability and handling restrictions remain subject to staff review.

access.accessMethod: British Library Digitisation Services order; quotation, preservation assessment, and exact reuse permission required
access.contact: British Library Digitisation Services; permissions@bl.uk for reuse permission
access.format: Manuscript photography in approved delivery formats
notes: For complete-object coverage, request covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, every unfoliated flyleaf and blank, both sides of every folio in the current object, edges where offered, final pastedown, and detached matter. Ask staff to confirm the full current foliation and image inventory because the interim catalog gives only '1 item'.
Map the work separately to ff. 138r-v, 173v, 213r-v, and 215v. Target-leaf crops alone are partial work-focused coverage, not a complete manuscript facsimile.
The service states that original manuscripts cannot be scanned; precious or rare items require photography. Reproduction and republication require the exact supplied terms and permissions.

rights.basis: Imaging Services requires an order, preservation assessment, payment, and written permission for copying or reproduction. No order or manuscript-specific license has been obtained.
rights.component: New British Library manuscript photography and supplied digital files
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and contractual delivery context

dateStatements.notes: This scholarly source date is retained separately from Wright's wording and the current whole-composite catalog range.

entities.description: A composite multilingual manuscript miscellany of receipts, medicine, charms, and other material. Wright's twelve selected craft units are assigned to six discontinuous loci: ff. 138r-v, 173v, 213r-v, and 215v. The current British Library record does not isolate those target units, hands, or local dates and gives only the whole composite's broad 10th-18th-century range.

holding.notes: All manuscripts and archives are consulted at the British Library in London. Current physical requests require a valid Reader Pass and the exact shelfmark.

entities.label: British Library, Sloane MS 73

physicalDescription.extent: 1 item in the current British Library record
notes: The current record lists Dutch, English, French, and Latin, with Latin script, and gives no target-specific support, dimensions, collation, or folio total.
Otho Polwhele owned the manuscript in 1641; William Balstone and P- Balstone are also recorded as former owners.
The record's listed contents at ff. 4-7b, 10-25, 26, 28-50b, 89-95, and 98-146b are broad selected descriptions and do not delimit Wright's target craft units.

rights.basis: The underlying verbal material is attested in a fifteenth-century manuscript and was published in 1844; no modern intellectual contribution is included in this component.
rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century Middle English verbal material represented by the twelve target units
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where historical copyright terms have expired

rights.basis: Imaging Services requires an order, preservation assessment, payment, and written permission for copying or reproduction. No order or manuscript-specific license has been obtained.
rights.component: New British Library manuscript photography and supplied digital files
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and contractual delivery context

bibliographic.citation: Mark Clarke, Tricks of the Medieval Trades: The Trinity Encyclopedia, a Collection of Fourteenth-Century English Craft Recipes (London: Archetype Publications, 2018), Appendix I, especially pp. 94-95.
bibliographic.title: Tricks of the Medieval Trades: The Trinity Encyclopedia, a Collection of Fourteenth-Century English Craft Recipes

entities.description: A modern translation and study of the Trinity Encyclopedia, a collection of seventy fourteenth-century English craft recipes, with Appendix I on Sloane MS 73. The official sample includes the T-S concordance and substantive variants needed here: Trinity section 1 corresponds to Sloane section C7, the target white-lead recipe on f. 213r, whose opening Clarke calls very compressed.
entities.label: Mark Clarke, Tricks of the Medieval Trades (2018)
notes: Only the official six-page publisher sample was publicly accessible and cached. It is partial coverage of the complete book.
The complete Appendix concordance may establish additional target mappings, but they are not inferred from the sampled rows alone.

rights.basis: The modern publication is offered commercially and the publisher site states all rights reserved; the public sample carries no open license.

access.accessMethod: Trinity College Cambridge James Catalogue record

entities.description: A five-part composite manuscript. Part I, dated by the current catalog to the fourteenth or fifteenth century, is an imperfect Book of Receipts in Middle English, mostly for colors and dyes. Clarke designates it MS T and relates its section 1 textually to Sloane MS S section C7. It is a comparative witness, not a demonstrated physical exemplar for Sloane MS 73.

holding.notes: Catalog access to the physical witness is recorded here. The Universal Viewer and IIIF image object are modeled separately as facsimile:trinity:O-9-39-complete-2024.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge, MS O.9.39

physicalDescription.extent: Five volumes bound together: I. 36 ff.; II. 12 ff.; III. 123 pp.; IV. 20 ff.; V. 40 ff.
notes: Part I is vellum, 49 lines per page, and imperfect at the beginning.
The catalog lists Italian, Latin, and Middle English for the composite.

access.accessMethod: Complete Universal Viewer and IIIF Presentation manifest
access.entityLabel: Complete Trinity College Cambridge digital facsimile of MS O.9.39
access.format: 323-canvas IIIF Presentation 2 object and Image API 2 JPEG services; 320 ordinary-light canvases plus three ultraviolet canvases

localCopies.label: Trinity College Cambridge IIIF manifest for O.9.39

workPortion.label: Comparative counterpart to the target Sloane f. 213r white-lead unit
locators: MS T section 1 corresponds textually to Sloane MS S section C7
notes: The cached manifest enumerates all 323 provider canvases but contains no image binaries; the separately listed IIIF ZIP retains every canvas image.
Direct inspection securely locates section 1 on provider page [1] p017 / canvas 21, beginning with the rubric 'For to make white leed' and ending before the next rubric on the same canvas.
No physical-source ancestry from O.9.39 to Sloane MS 73 is asserted.

localCopies.label: Complete Trinity College Cambridge MS O.9.39 IIIF facsimile bundle
notes: Complete provider inventory of 323 canvases: canvases 1-320 form the ordinary-light object sequence from front cover through back cover, and canvases 321-323 are the provider's ultraviolet repetitions of [1] p001, p016, and p033.

The requested maximum-width profile was IIIF size segment 1600,. It was used for 315 canvases; eight narrow Part IV stub canvases (219-220, 223-224, 231-232, and 235-236) were retained at full native width, 772 or 774 pixels, instead of being upscaled.
ZIP contains the exact provider manifest, a deterministic exact-source inventory with per-image byte counts and SHA-256 values, and all 323 JPEG derivatives.

workPortion.label: Comparative counterpart to the target Sloane f. 213r white-lead unit
locators: MS T section 1 corresponds textually to Sloane MS S section C7
notes: Direct inspection of p017 and its adjacent canvases securely bounds T section 1 to p017 alone: it begins with the rubric 'For to make white leed' and ends before the following rubric on the same canvas.
The p016 and p018 images were inspected only to confirm the boundary; they are not included in the mapped textual counterpart.
No physical-source ancestry from O.9.39 to Sloane MS 73 is asserted.
The catalog reports that currently displayed images were recaptured at higher resolution in October 2024.
The ignored local ZIP is a private research cache under the provider's CC BY-NC 4.0 license; public availability and redistribution are separate questions.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest declares CC BY-NC 4.0, and the manuscript catalog page states that Trinity's work is copyright the Master and Fellows of Trinity College, Cambridge and licensed under the same terms.
rights.component: The complete 323-canvas Trinity College photographic facsimile of MS O.9.39, including the three ultraviolet canvases
notes: This record is scoped to the photographic digital facsimile, not the physical witness or the underlying medieval text.
The catalog record remains a separate witness-access route and is not conflated with the licensed image object.
The complete local bundle is Git-ignored research evidence; redistribution must comply with CC BY-NC 4.0 and corpus publication choices remain separate from cache availability.

entities.description: Trinity College Cambridge's complete published 323-canvas digital object for the five-part composite MS O.9.39. The sequence contains 320 ordinary-light object canvases from the front cover through the back cover, followed by three ultraviolet canvases for Part I provider pages p001, p016, and p033.
entities.label: Complete Trinity College Cambridge digital facsimile of MS O.9.39
notes: Completeness is relative to all 323 canvases in the provider's published manifest, not an inference that every physically possible exterior or analytical image exists.
The three ultraviolet canvases repeat provider pages [1] p001, p016, and p033 in another imaging mode and remain part of the complete provider object.
Direct inspection securely maps Clarke's MS T section 1 only to provider page [1] p017 / IIIF canvas 21; no other canvas is assigned to that textual counterpart.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest declares CC BY-NC 4.0, and the manuscript catalog page states that Trinity's work is copyright the Master and Fellows of Trinity College, Cambridge and licensed under the same terms.
rights.component: The complete 323-canvas Trinity College photographic facsimile of MS O.9.39, including the three ultraviolet canvases
notes: This record is scoped to the photographic digital facsimile, not the physical witness or the underlying medieval text.
The catalog record remains a separate witness-access route and is not conflated with the licensed image object.
The complete local bundle is Git-ignored research evidence; redistribution must comply with CC BY-NC 4.0 and corpus publication choices remain separate from cache availability.

next_steps: Request the complete current physical object, not only target leaves, and require a supplied image inventory tied to current foliation.
Map the work separately to ff. 138r-v, 173v, 213r-v, and 215v, with adjacent surfaces for boundary analysis.
Retain quotation, correspondence, delivered checksums, and exact manuscript-specific license; do not describe an order as pending or permission as granted until an actual request receives a response.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply or authorize a complete color digital facsimile of the entire current physical Sloane MS 73, and what exact storage, Git-publication, viewer, print-on-demand, and commercial permissions would govern it?

next_steps: Inspect complete target and adjacent manuscript surfaces and make a line-by-line concordance with Wright.
Apply MEG-C L4708 only to f. 138r-v unless palaeographic evidence extends that hand.
Keep Wright's earlier-fifteenth-century date, MED's a1475 stencil, and the current broad whole-composite range separately attributed.
openQuestions.question: What exact manuscript boundaries, neighboring texts, hands, sequence positions, and local dates correspond to all twelve target units?

next_steps: Collate every printed unit, rubric, folio label, note, and the jordan figure against manuscript images.
Investigate the printed 'etc.' in the fine-azure recipe without assigning a cause in advance.
Determine whether Wright used the codex directly or an intermediary transcript.
openQuestions.question: What selection, normalization, expansion, punctuation, correction, omission, or reordering did Wright apply to the manuscript readings?

next_steps: Consult Clarke's complete 2018 Appendix I and 2016 edition for target-specific concordances and variants.
Retain the current white-lead C7/T1 relationship without extrapolating it to other target recipes.
Separate abstract textual versions and reconstructed collections from physical witnesses and direct-copy claims.
openQuestions.question: Which remaining target units have secure counterparts in the Trinity Encyclopedia or other craft-recipe traditions, and what relationship can be demonstrated?

next_steps: Consult original issue wrappers, publisher records, and authoritative serial bibliography.
Preserve article and journal-volume year 1844 separately from completed bound title-page year 1845.
Do not synthesize the two layers into one unqualified publication date.
openQuestions.question: What exact issue date and wrapper history explains an 1844 article and journal volume inside a completed bound carrier dated 1845?

relations.subjectLabel: Complete Trinity College Cambridge digital facsimile of MS O.9.39

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00136.xml. The U-M encoding, Wright article, completed bound carrier, Internet Archive scan, Sloane witness, catalog descriptions, and later scholarship are separate entities.
The cataloged historical unit is Wright's exact twelve-unit edited selection, not one continuous medieval codex section, all craft recipes in Sloane MS 73, or the reconstructed Trinity Encyclopedia.

The seven XML DIV2 elements represent one Wright introduction plus six manuscript-folio groups; fourteen medieval content paragraphs represent twelve recipe/advice units because two units split rubric from procedure.

The current British Library record's 0900-1799 range is for the entire composite and is not target-folio dating. Wright's earlier-fifteenth-century statement and MED's (a1475) stencil remain separately attributed.
MEG-C entry L4708 applies to the scribal text on ff. 95v-146. It can therefore contextualize target f. 138r-v only and is not generalized to ff. 173v, 213r-v, or 215v.

The current British Library record has an empty Digitised Content field and exact-shelfmark searches exposed no authorized complete public target-manuscript viewer or IIIF object as of 2026-07-13. This dated negative finding does not prove unpublished, internal, or legacy photography does not exist.

Trinity College MS O.9.39 is a public comparative physical witness, while Trinity's 323-canvas IIIF object is a separate digital facsimile with its own image access and rights. Clarke's concordance supports a textual-version relation for the white-lead portions only; direct image inspection maps that portion to provider page [1] p017 / canvas 21, and O.9.39 is not modeled as a physical ancestor of Sloane MS 73.
The pinned CME root LICENSE uses Unlicense language framed as software. Its application to XML/data/markup remains unresolved and is not broadened into a high-confidence rights claim.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository representation and version of University of Michigan CME00136, which keys Thomas Wright's complete four-page article 'Early English Artistical Receipts' in the Archaeological Journal 1 (1844), pp. 152-155. Wright prints a modern introduction and twelve Middle English technical recipe/advice units from six discontinuous Sloane MS 73 loci, out of folio order: ff. 173v, 138v, 138r, 213r, 213v, and 215v. The article-year layer is 1844, while the completed bound carrier's title page is dated MDCCCXLV (1845); both are retained. A complete Internet Archive scan of that bound carrier and its OCR, metadata, page map, and scandata are cached with fixity. The current British Library record is a broad whole-composite description, leaves Digitised Content blank, and exposes no authorized public complete Sloane MS 73 image object as of 2026-07-13; onsite consultation and paid imaging remain available routes. MED indexes the selected text as (a1475) Recipe Painting(2), and MEG-C localizes only the target f. 138r-v hand to Bedfordshire. Clarke's 2018 concordance securely relates the target white-lead unit to Trinity Encyclopedia section 1 without establishing physical copying direction; the distinct complete 323-canvas Trinity digital facsimile is cached at a 1600-pixel request profile, and direct inspection locates that counterpart on provider page [1] p017 / IIIF canvas 21 only.

catalogSummary.title: Early English Artistical Receipts (Wright's 1844 selection from Sloane MS 73)

catalogingSubject.label: Early English Artistical Receipts (Wright's 1844 selection from Sloane MS 73)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact editorial selection of twelve Middle English technical recipe or practical-advice units that Thomas Wright printed from British Library Sloane MS 73 on pp. 152-155 of the Archaeological Journal in 1844 and that U-M encoded as CME00136. Wright drew them from six discontinuous manuscript loci and printed the loci out of folio order: ff. 173v, 138v, 138r, 213r, 213v, and 215v. The subject is not one securely bounded medieval authored work, not every craft recipe in Sloane MS 73, not Wright's modern-English introduction and notes, and not the reconstructed Trinity Encyclopedia. The manuscript witness, 1844 article, completed 1845 bound carrier, scan, U-M encoding, repository XML, and later scholarship remain separate lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Thomas Wright's editorial introduction

locators.value: p. 152, opening paragraph below EARLY ENGLISH ARTISTICAL RECEIPTS

notes: Editorial paratext, not one of the twelve medieval items and excluded from intrinsic-work counts and language facets.
contentParts.summary: A modern-English paragraph identifies Sloane MS 73, dates it to the earlier fifteenth century, compares its color recipes with documents printed in the journal's first number, and singles out the glass-softening recipe as curious.

contentParts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 173v: colored wax

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 170 / Internet Archive leaf n172

contentParts.summary: Two recipes for red and green wax using white wax, turpentine, and vermilion or verdigris.

contentParts.label: For to make reed wex

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Mixes white wax and turpentine, tests the gumminess in cold water, and colors the melted mixture with finely ground vermilion.

contentParts.label: For to make grene wex

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Adapts the red-wax method by coloring the wax and turpentine mixture with ground verdigris.

contentParts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 138v: vermilion

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF pages 170-171 / Internet Archive leaves n172-n173

contentParts.summary: One long two-paragraph recipe for manufacturing vermilion from quicksilver and brimstone, with furnace directions, color signs, and safety advice.

contentParts.label: Here it techith how thou schalt make good vermyloun

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

notes: The two P elements are one rubric-plus-procedure unit, not two recipes. Wright's footnotes a-c and the reproduced marginal jordan figure are editorial apparatus.
contentParts.summary: Combines quicksilver and brimstone, describes enclosed heating and sublimation in a luted glass jordan, uses changes in flame color to judge progress, and warns the maker to eat and drink before encountering the harmful fumes.

contentParts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 138r: verdigris

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 171 / Internet Archive leaf n173

contentParts.summary: One two-paragraph recipe for making verdigris from copper filings and vinegar in a sealed vessel surrounded by horse dung.

contentParts.label: Here it techith how thou schalt make fyn vertgrece and good

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

notes: The two P elements are one rubric-plus-procedure unit, not two recipes.
contentParts.summary: Specifies copper-to-vinegar proportions and two months of sealed incubation in a measured surround of horse dung.

contentParts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 213r: lead and copper pigments

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 172 / Internet Archive leaf n174

contentParts.summary: Three recipes for white lead, red lead, and verdigris.

contentParts.label: For to make whit leed

locators.value: f. 213r; Clarke MS S section C7

notes: Clarke identifies the opening of Sloane section C7 as a very compressed version corresponding to Trinity section 1. This is a textual counterpart, not evidence that Sloane MS 73 copied directly from the surviving Trinity College manuscript.
contentParts.summary: Suspends new lead plates above red-wine lees in a sealed vessel kept in hot horse dung, then scrapes, washes, dries, and optionally forms the white-lead product into saleable pieces.

contentParts.label: Now for to make reed leed

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Grinds dried white lead to powder and heats it in an earthen pot with repeated stirring until the desired red color appears.

contentParts.label: For to make vertegrece

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Suspends copper plates over vinegar or strong lees in a warm sealed vessel, repeatedly scrapes the green product, grinds it with vinegar, and presses and dries it in leather.

contentParts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 213v: multiplying verdigris

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 173 / Internet Archive leaf n175

contentParts.summary: One recipe for repeatedly increasing prepared verdigris with copper filings and strong vinegar.

contentParts.label: To multiplie vertegrece

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Mixes Spanish verdigris with copper filings and strong vinegar, incubates the sealed mixture in hot horse dung, and repeats the process with fresh filings and vinegar.

contentParts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 215v: glass and azure

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 173 / Internet Archive leaf n175

contentParts.summary: Four units on softening glass, preparing azure from lapis lazuli without paste, recognizing and testing lapis lazuli, and making azure bice.

contentParts.label: For to make glas nesche

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Boils glass with warmed goat's blood and mustard juice so that it becomes soft and resists breaking when thrown against a wall.

contentParts.label: For to make fyn azure withoute past

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Dissolves sal ammoniac, heats and quenches whole lapis lazuli, washes away the salt, and dries the resulting color.

contentParts.label: How to recognize and assay lapis lazuli

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

notes: The supplied English label describes an unheaded advice and testing unit; it is not presented as a manuscript rubric.
contentParts.summary: Describes true lapis lazuli by its fine blue color and gold-like streaks or specks, rejects visibly gravelly stones, and distinguishes it from lapis almanie by heating, cooling, color, hardness, and brittleness.

contentParts.label: On this manere thu myȝt make azure bis

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

contentParts.summary: Repeatedly grinds white lead or ceruse with the juice of a blue flower growing in corn, drying between grindings until the desired blue color is reached.

assertion.notes: Witness dating and textual parallels do not establish composition dates for the individual recipes.

dateStatements.display: Unknown; the twelve constituent units may have different origins and may predate their surviving witness

assertion.notes: The current British Library record's 0900-1799 date range applies to the whole composite and cannot date these target folios. MED's a1475 source date and Wright's earlier-fifteenth-century description are preserved as distinct scholarly assertions.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth century; MED indexes Wright's selected text as (a1475), while Wright described the manuscript as written in the earlier part of the fifteenth century
notes: Individual recipes or textual traditions may be older than their Sloane witness.

scope.description: The selected units' survival in Sloane MS 73, not a confirmed date for one autonomous compilation

assertion.notes: The completed bound volume has a separate 1845 title-page imprint; that carrier date does not replace the article and journal-volume year 1844.

dateStatements.display: 1844 (first known publication of this exact twelve-unit selection)

extent.description: The cataloged medieval selection contains twelve unnumbered prose recipe/advice units at six discontinuous folio loci. In the XML these appear beneath six document DIV2s, plus a seventh DIV2 for Wright's editorial introduction. Fourteen medieval content P elements represent twelve units because the f. 138v vermilion and f. 138r verdigris units each split a rubric from its procedural paragraph. Six HEADNOTE/P elements supply folio labels and one further P is Wright's introduction, yielding 21 P elements overall. The selection occupies four printed pages, pp. 152-155.

formStatements.description: All twelve medieval units are prose recipes or practical advice. The XML contains no intrinsic verse groups or lines.

assertion.notes: The six folio loci are printed discontinuously and out of manuscript folio order.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in Wright's heading, introduction, folio labels, footnotes, and signature belongs to the 1844 editorial layer and is not a language facet of the twelve-unit medieval cataloging subject.
The preferred title is supplied for disambiguation; Wright's all-capital printed heading is retained separately.

The twelve medieval units, fourteen medieval content paragraphs, six document DIV2s, seven total DIV2s, six folio loci, and four printed pages are distinct metrics and are not substituted for one another.
The current British Library record is a broad interim whole-composite description. Its 10th-18th-century range and listed contents do not isolate the target craft recipes.

The MED bibliography note for Sloane 73 ff. 196-202 concerns another hand and source stream; its only target use here is the explicit cross-reference to (a1475) Recipe Painting(2) in Archaeological Journal 1.

A complete target-manuscript facsimile should cover the entire current physical Sloane MS 73 object and map the twelve selected units separately; target-leaf crops alone would not constitute complete manuscript coverage.

next_steps: Inspect complete color images of every target recto and verso plus adjacent surfaces.
Build a line-by-line concordance against Wright and record whether rubrics and boundaries are manuscript features.
Do not infer continuity from Wright's compact four-page presentation or treat the broad current BL contents ranges as target extent.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact starts, ends, neighboring texts, hands, and original sequence positions of all twelve units on Sloane MS 73 ff. 138r-v, 173v, 213r-v, and 215v?

next_steps: Keep Wright's earlier-fifteenth-century statement and MED's a1475 indexing as separate source assertions.
Apply MEG-C's Bedfordshire L4708 association only to the hand spanning ff. 95v-146 and therefore target f. 138r-v.
Consult complete images, current codicological descriptions, IMEP records, and recipe scholarship before dating or localizing the other target folios.
openQuestions.question: When and where were the individual recipe units composed and copied, and how many scribal hands are represented across the six loci?

next_steps: Retain only Clarke's secure target correspondence between Sloane C7 and Trinity section 1 in the current record.
Consult Clarke's complete 2018 concordance and 2016 edition for target-specific section mappings and variants.
Model textual versions or parallels separately from surviving physical manuscripts and do not infer direct copying without evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which of the other eleven target units correspond to Trinity Encyclopedia sections or other craft-recipe traditions, and what direction or stemmatic relationship, if any, can be established?

next_steps: Collate Wright's text, rubrics, folio labels, notes, and vessel figure directly against manuscript images.
Investigate the printed 'etc.' in the fine-azure recipe without assuming it marks manuscript text, omission, or damage.
Do not treat U-M's faithful encoding of print as independent verification of Wright's readings.
openQuestions.question: What normalization, punctuation, expansion, omission, reordering, or silent correction did Wright apply to the Sloane readings?

next_steps: Consult original issue wrappers, publisher records, and authoritative serial bibliography.
Continue to record article and journal year 1844 separately from bound-carrier title-page year 1845.
Do not collapse the two date layers into a synthesized single publication date.
openQuestions.question: What exact issue date and issue-wrapper history produced the 1844 article within a completed volume whose bound title page is dated 1845?

assertion.notes: The selected texts are Middle English and circulate in an English manuscript context; no narrower common place of composition or compilation is established.
This is a scribal-language association. It is not transferred to f. 173v, ff. 213r-v, f. 215v, authorial origin, or the place where Wright formed his selection.

scope.description: Only the two target units on f. 138r-v, within the hand MEG-C defines as ff. 95v-146

assertion.notes: Anonymous is a display responsibility, not a claim that one unidentified person composed all twelve units.

scope.description: The twelve constituent recipe and advice units, which need not share one author

assertion.notes: The closing signature T. WRIGHT. confirms responsibility for the article; the exact degree of normalization and intervention remains unresolved.

scope.description: The complete four-page 1844 printed selection and its modern apparatus

assertion.notes: Wright says the receipts are taken from Sloane MS 73, but does not document whether he worked directly from the codex or through an intermediary transcript.

scope.description: The selected manuscript readings printed from Sloane MS 73

summaries.value: Twelve practical prose units explain how to color wax red and green; manufacture vermilion, verdigris, white lead, and red lead; multiply verdigris; soften glass; prepare azure from lapis lazuli; distinguish true lapis lazuli from lapis almanie by visual and heat tests; and make azure bice from white lead and the juice of a blue cornfield flower. The recipes emphasize proportions, vessels, heat, manure incubation, repeated processing, signs of completion, and tests of material quality.

Wright prints the medieval material in six folio-labeled groups containing 2, 1, 1, 3, 1, and 4 units respectively. CME00136 has seven DIV2 elements because a modern-English editorial introduction precedes those six document groups. Its 21 P elements comprise one introduction paragraph, six folio-headnote paragraphs, and fourteen medieval content paragraphs; the vermilion and f. 138r verdigris recipes each use separate rubric and instruction paragraphs, so fourteen content paragraphs represent twelve recipe/advice units rather than fourteen works.

The record models Wright's exact selection as an edited selection and retains twelve internal units for description and concordance. It does not infer that the printed order reproduces a continuous manuscript sequence, transfer Sloane 73's whole-codex date range to the target folios, treat the related Trinity Encyclopedia as a physical source, or count Wright's introduction, twelve footnotes, marginal vessel figure, or six folio headnotes as medieval works.

assertion.notes: The witness, editor, and year qualifier distinguishes this exact discontinuous selection from other craft recipes in Sloane MS 73 and from the related Trinity Encyclopedia; it is not a manuscript title.

titles.value: Early English Artistical Receipts (Wright's 1844 selection from Sloane MS 73)

notes: This is Wright's printed heading, not evidence for an original manuscript title or rubric.

scope.description: The first medieval unit in Wright's printed order
The ending of the final medieval unit in Wright's printed order
